content.text: Writing Your First Function Component
A React function component is a JavaScript function that accepts a
props
object and returns JSX describing what to render. That is the entire definition. No class, no special registration, no framework magic — just a function.

attrs.alt: Anatomy of a React function component
attrs.title: The five key rules labelled on a real component

content.text: The Simplest Possible Component
Here is the smallest complete React component — no props, no state, just a function that returns JSX:
To use it anywhere in your app:
Each
<Hello />
is an independent instance. React calls the function once per instance and renders the returned JSX into the DOM. That is the component model in its entirety at this level.
The Five Rules You Must Know
1. Name Starts with a Capital Letter

React distinguishes between HTML elements and components by capitalisation. Lowercase names are treated as native DOM elements; capitalised names are treated as component references that React will call.

<div>     // native HTML element — rendered directly to the DOM
<Greeting // component — React calls the Greeting function
<greeting // BUG: React treats this as an unknown HTML element
2. Accepts a Single Props Object
React passes all JSX attributes as a single
props
object to the function. You can receive it as-is or destructure it inline:
Props can be strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, objects, or functions — any JavaScript value. String props use quotes; all other values use curly braces
3. Returns JSX (or null)
The function must return a single root JSX element,
null
(renders nothing), or a fragment. Returning two sibling elements directly is a syntax error:
4. Use className, not class
JSX is transpiled to JavaScript, and
class
is a reserved word in JavaScript. All JSX attributes follow camelCase JavaScript naming, with a few exceptions:
HTML attribute
JSX equivalent
Reason
class
className
class is a reserved JS keyword
for
htmlFor
for is a reserved JS keyword
tabindex
tabIndex
camelCase convention
onclick
onClick
camelCase convention
style="color: red"
style={{ color: 'red' }}
Object with camelCase properties
5. Curly Braces Embed JavaScript Expressions
Inside JSX,
switches from JSX back to JavaScript. Any
expression
(something that evaluates to a value) is valid inside braces. Statements (if, for, while) are not — use ternaries and array methods instead:
Arrow Function Syntax
Arrow functions work equally well as component definitions. Both syntaxes are in common use:

Function declarations are hoisted — you can use them before they are defined in the file. Arrow function expressions are not hoisted. For top-level components this rarely matters, but it is worth knowing.
Default Props with Destructuring Defaults
Provide default values for optional props using JavaScript destructuring defaults — no special React API needed:
A boolean prop with no value (just the name) is equivalent to
disabled={true}
— the same shorthand works in HTML.
The children Prop
JSX content placed
between
opening and closing tags is automatically passed as the
children
prop. This is how you build container components:
children can be any renderable React node: a string, a number, another JSX element, an array of elements, or
null
. Components that expect children are called
container components
or
wrapper components
and form the basis of React's composition model.
Self-Closing Tags
In JSX, all elements
must be closed
. HTML is lenient about this (a lone
is valid HTML), but JSX is strict XML-like syntax. Use self-closing syntax for elements with no children:

// WRONG in JSX
// Correct JSX
// Same rule applies to custom components with no children
Exporting and Importing Components
React doesn't have a special component registry — components are just JavaScript modules. Use standard ES module exports:
By convention, each component lives in its own file named to match the component (
Greeting.jsx
exports
Greeting
). This is a convention, not a requirement — multiple small components in one file is fine.
Composing Components
Components compose by nesting — you build a complex UI from simple pieces. React renders the tree top-down, calling each component function in order:
Each component only knows about its direct props.
Avatar
does not know it is inside a
ProfileCard
. This isolation makes components easy to test, reuse, and reason about in isolation.
Common Mistakes When Writing Your First Component
Forgetting to Export
Calling a Component as a Function
Calling a component as a function makes hooks inside it unreliable — React cannot track state or effects for a component that is not part of the React tree.
Returning Multiple Root Elements
Mutating Props
Props are owned by the parent. A component that mutates them breaks the unidirectional data flow and can cause subtle bugs where the parent component has a stale view of its own state.
Conditional Rendering Patterns
Since JSX is just JavaScript, all conditional logic uses standard JS operators inside curly braces. Three patterns cover most cases:
Avoid the pattern {count && <Item />} when count might be 0 — React renders the number 0 to the DOM, producing unexpected output. Use {count > 0 && <Item />} or a ternary instead.
Rendering Lists
Render arrays by mapping them to JSX elements. Each element in a list must have a unique
key
prop — React uses this to efficiently update only the changed items:
Inline Styles vs className
React supports both CSS classes and inline styles. Inline styles use a JavaScript object with camelCase property names and string values (or numeric where the unit is px):

For most styling, prefer CSS classes over inline styles — they are easier to override, support media queries, and allow browser devtools to inspect them. Inline styles are appropriate for values that must be computed at runtime (e.g. a progress bar width based on a percentage).
A Complete Working Example
Here is a realistic component that combines everything covered in this document — props with defaults, conditional rendering, a list, composition, and proper exports:
Summary
A function component is a JavaScript function that takes props and returns JSX.
Names must start with a capital letter; the return must be a single root element or fragment.
Use
className
not
class
embeds any JavaScript expression in JSX.
Props are read-only; never mutate them inside the component.
children is a built-in prop — content between tags — enabling container/wrapper components.
Export components with ES module syntax; import them by name or as default.
Render lists with
.map()
and always provide a unique
key
prop from stable data.
